beginner.question: What is a screen reader and how does it work with web content?
beginner.answer: A screen reader is assistive technology that converts digital text to speech or braille. It reads the DOM's accessibility tree — a subset of elements with semantic meaning — and allows users to navigate by element type (headings, links, landmarks, form controls) using keyboard shortcuts.

beginner.question: What are the most popular screen readers?
beginner.answer: NVDA (Windows, free/open-source), JAWS (Windows, commercial), VoiceOver (macOS/iOS, built-in), Narrator (Windows, built-in), and TalkBack (Android, built-in). Each has slightly different behavior and keyboard shortcuts.

beginner.question: How do screen reader users navigate a webpage?
beginner.answer: They use browse mode (virtual cursor) to read content and navigate by element type using single-key shortcuts: H for headings, L for links, D for landmarks, T for tables, B for buttons, etc. Tab moves between focusable elements. Quick navigation keys vary by screen reader.

beginner.question: What is the difference between browse mode and focus mode?
beginner.answer: Browse mode lets users navigate and read content using virtual cursor and quick navigation keys. Focus mode passes keyboard input directly to the page, used inside interactive elements like forms and ARIA widgets. NVDA toggles with NVDA+Space.

beginner.question: How do you make images accessible to screen readers?
beginner.answer: Use the alt attribute: informative images get descriptive alt text, decorative images get alt="" plus role="presentation", complex images get alt text plus a detailed description nearby or in a <figcaption>.

beginner.question: Why is heading hierarchy important?
beginner.answer: Screen reader users navigate by headings to understand page structure. A logical hierarchy (h1 > h2 > h3) creates a document outline. Skipping levels or using headings for visual styling only confuses navigation.

beginner.question: What makes link text accessible?
beginner.answer: Link text must describe the destination when read out of context. Avoid vague text like 'click here' or 'read more'. If a link has no visible text (icon link), provide an aria-label with the destination description.

beginner.question: How do form labels help screen reader users?
beginner.answer: Labels programmatically associate descriptive text with form controls using the for/id attributes. Screen readers announce the label when the input receives focus, telling users what information to enter.

beginner.question: What are ARIA landmarks and why are they important?
beginner.answer: ARIA landmarks (role="navigation", "main", "banner", "complementary", "contentinfo") define page regions. Screen reader users can jump between landmarks using keyboard shortcuts, enabling quick access to different sections of the page.

beginner.question: What is the NVDA elements list?
beginner.answer: NVDA+F7 opens the elements list dialog showing all headings, links, landmarks, and form controls on the page. Users can filter by type and navigate directly to any element without tabbing through the page.

intermediate.question: How do you handle dynamic content changes for screen readers?
intermediate.answer: Use aria-live regions with appropriate politeness: aria-live="polite" for non-critical updates (chat messages), aria-live="assertive" for urgent updates (errors). role="alert" provides assertive behavior. Ensure the region is empty on load to avoid announcing initial content.

intermediate.question: What is the role of the accessibility tree in screen reader support?
intermediate.answer: The accessibility tree is a subset of the DOM that browsers expose to assistive technologies. It includes elements with semantic meaning, ARIA roles, and text content. Elements hidden with aria-hidden or display:none are excluded. Screen readers use this tree to generate speech output.

intermediate.question: How do you handle screen reader announcements for loading states?
intermediate.answer: Use aria-busy="true" on the loading region to indicate ongoing updates. Add role="status" or aria-live="polite" regions for status updates. Announce completion with a polite live region or role="status". For spinners, add aria-label="Loading content" and aria-valuetext as appropriate.

intermediate.question: What is the VoiceOver Rotor and how is it used?
intermediate.answer: VoiceOver Rotor (VO+U) provides a radial menu for quick navigation by headings, links, landmarks, form controls, tables, and more. Users can select a category and flick up/down to navigate. Custom rotor items can be added with WebKit-specific ARIA attributes.

intermediate.question: How do you make single-page application routes accessible to screen readers?
intermediate.answer: After route changes: manage focus by moving it to the main content area or a heading within the new content. Use aria-live="polite" to announce the new page title. Ensure the page title updates. Maintain proper heading hierarchy. Handle loading/failed states with appropriate announcements.

intermediate.question: What are common screen reader testing pitfalls?
intermediate.answer: Only testing with one screen reader (differences between NVDA/JAWS/VoiceOver), testing with familiar content (miss issues real users would face), not testing keyboard-only navigation, ignoring dynamic content announcements, assuming ARIA works the same across screen readers, and testing late in development.

intermediate.question: How do you test screen reader support for custom ARIA widgets?
intermediate.answer: 1. Verify roles/states in accessibility inspector. 2. Test keyboard navigation flows. 3. Listen to announcements: does the screen reader announce the role, state, and name correctly? 4. Test all states (expanded, collapsed, disabled, selected). 5. Test with multiple screen readers. 6. Get feedback from screen reader users.

intermediate.question: How do screen readers handle CSS-generated content?
intermediate.answer: Screen reader support for CSS ::before and ::after content varies. In general, CSS content is not reliably announced. Never use CSS content to convey information that isn't also in the HTML. Use HTML content or ARIA attributes instead.

intermediate.question: What is forms mode and when does it activate?
intermediate.answer: Forms mode (also called focus mode or application mode) activates when focus enters a form control or ARIA widget. In this mode, keyboard input passes directly to the element — quick navigation keys (H, L, D) are disabled. Users must exit forms mode (NVDA+Space or Tab out) to resume browse navigation.

intermediate.question: How do you handle screen reader announcements for drag-and-drop interfaces?
intermediate.answer: Use aria-grabbed for drag state, aria-dropeffect for drop targets. Announce drag start/end and drop success/failure via aria-live regions. Provide clear instructions (aria-describedby). Implement keyboard alternatives that make the operation apparent through announcements at each step.

advanced.question: How do different screen readers handle the accessible name computation differently?
advanced.answer: While the specification defines the order (aria-labelledby > aria-label > native > content > title), implementations vary. JAWS sometimes uses title when it shouldn't. VoiceOver may ignore aria-label on some elements. NVDA generally follows the spec. Testing across all three is essential for reliable accessible names.

advanced.question: How do you handle screen reader testing for canvas-based applications?
advanced.answer: Canvas content is invisible to screen readers. Use fallback content between canvas tags, provide off-screen DOM representations with ARIA attributes, implement focusable canvas elements with keyboard events, or use SVG with accessible descriptions instead of canvas for interactive graphics.

advanced.question: What are the accessibility implications of Shadow DOM v1 for screen readers?
advanced.answer: Shadow DOM can create accessibility challenges because shadow boundaries affect traversal. ARIA relationships (aria-labelledby, aria-describedby) across shadow boundaries work in modern browsers using slot references. DelegatesFocus helps with focus management. Screen reader support for Shadow DOM ARIA varies by browser.

advanced.question: How do you implement a custom screen reader test automation strategy?
advanced.answer: Combine unit tests (jest-axe, axe-core for programmatic checks), integration tests (cypress-axe for interaction testing), and manual screen reader testing. Use tools like axe-core to validate the accessibility tree structure. Create test scripts for screen reader users to follow, documenting expected announcements at each step.

advanced.question: How does hydration of server-rendered content affect screen readers?
advanced.answer: During hydration, there's a period where server-rendered static HTML is replaced by dynamic React/Vue/Angular content. If ARIA attributes or live regions are only added client-side, screen readers may miss initial announcements. Solution: include ARIA in server-rendered HTML or use SSR-compatible accessibility patterns.

advanced.question: How do screen readers handle infinite scroll and virtualized lists?
advanced.answer: Infinite scroll challenges screen readers because new content loads without traditional navigation. Solutions: use role="feed" for continuous content, aria-setsize and aria-posinset for position context, announce new items via aria-live, provide 'load more' buttons as alternatives, and manage focus when content loads above the current position.

advanced.question: What is the NVDA add-on ecosystem for accessibility testing?
advanced.answer: NVDA has add-ons that enhance testing: 'Focus Highlight' for visual focus tracking, 'Resource Monitor' for performance, 'Accessibility Viewer' for inspecting accessibility tree, and custom speech dictionaries. Custom add-ons can be developed in Python for specific testing needs.

advanced.question: How do you handle screen reader support for real-time collaborative editing?
advanced.answer: Use aria-live="polite" regions for remote user activity announcements. Announce cursor position changes ("User X is editing paragraph 3"). Manage focus carefully to avoid interrupting the user's work. Use aria-relevant="additions text" to announce content changes. Implement edit summaries: "User X added a sentence."

advanced.question: What are the differences in screen reader behavior between browsers?
advanced.answer: Chrome + NVDA has good ARIA support. Firefox + NVDA historically has the best accessibility tree support. Safari + VoiceOver is the most integrated pair. Edge + Narrator uses UIA API for better support. JAWS works best with Chrome. Each combination interprets ARIA slightly differently, requiring cross-browser testing.

advanced.question: How do you optimize page performance without sacrificing screen reader support?
advanced.answer: Lazy-load content with aria-busy management. Virtualize lists with proper aria-setsize/aria-posinset. Defer non-critical JavaScript but ensure critical accessibility features (keyboard handling, ARIA attributes) load first. Use CSS content-visibility for off-screen sections but ensure they're in the accessibility tree. Avoid removing elements from the DOM if they provide navigation context.
